Cache-aside (Lazy loading) — The application manages the cache itself. It always checks the cache first. On a miss it loads from DB and populates the cache. The cache never holds data that hasn't been requested at least once.
Read path
App
▶
1. check cache
Cache (Redis)
MISS → null
▶
2. read DB
Database
returns data
◀
3. populate cache
Cache (Redis)
SET key, ttl
Step by step
1
App checks cache — GET key
Before any DB call, app does cache.get(key). Returns immediately if found.
M
Cache miss — key not found or expired
On null response, app falls through to the real data source (DB / external service).
3
App reads DB, gets the data
Normal repository/SQL call. This is the slow path (~100–500ms).
4
App writes result to cache — SET key value EX ttl
Result is now in cache. All subsequent reads for this key hit cache until TTL expires.
H
Next read — cache hit (<5ms)
Key found; DB is never called. All reads until TTL expiry are served from cache.
Java — manual cache-aside with Redis
public Product getProduct(Long id) {
  String key = "product:" + id;

  // 1. Check cache first
  Product cached = redisTemplate.opsForValue().get(key);
  if (cached != null) return cached;  // cache HIT → return instantly

  // 2. Cache MISS → load from DB
  Product product = productRepo.findById(id)
      .orElseThrow(NotFoundException::new);

  // 3. Populate cache with TTL
  redisTemplate.opsForValue().set(key, product, Duration.ofMinutes(10));
  return product;
}

// Invalidate on update — caller is responsible
public Product updateProduct(Product p) {
  Product saved = productRepo.save(p);
  redisTemplate.delete("product:" + p.getId());  // evict stale entry
  return saved;
}
Advantages
+Cache only holds what was actually requested — no wasted memory
+Cache failure doesn't break reads — app falls back to DB gracefully
+Most flexible — works with any data source
Drawbacks
–Cache miss penalty — first read is always slow
–Cache stampede: many threads miss simultaneously and all hit DB
–App code must manually manage eviction on writes
Write-through — Every write goes to the cache AND the database synchronously, in the same operation. Cache is always consistent with DB. Reads always hit the cache.
Write path
App
▶
1. write
Cache (Redis)
SET immediately
▶
2. write (sync)
Database
persisted
◀
3. confirm
App
returns result
Step by step
1
App writes to cache first (or simultaneously)
Cache is updated before the DB call returns. Cache is never stale.
2
App writes to DB synchronously
Both writes happen in the same request/transaction. Caller waits for both to complete.
3
All subsequent reads hit cache
No cold-start miss problem. Cache is pre-warmed on every write. Read path is always fast.
Java — Spring @CachePut (write-through)
// @CachePut: ALWAYS executes the method AND updates the cache
// Unlike @Cacheable, it never skips the method call
@CachePut(value = "products", key = "#product.id")
public Product save(Product product) {
  return productRepo.save(product);
  // return value is what gets stored in cache
  // cache key = product.id, value = saved Product
}

// Read — always a cache hit (data was written through)
@Cacheable(value = "products", key = "#id")
public Product findById(Long id) {
  return productRepo.findById(id).orElseThrow();
  // DB only hit on very first read before any write
}

// Combined: save + immediately readable from cache
public void demo() {
  Product p = save(newProduct);   // writes DB + cache
  Product q = findById(p.getId()); // cache HIT — no DB call
}
Advantages
+Cache always consistent with DB — no stale reads
+No cold-start penalty — reads always hit the cache
+Simple read path — always read from cache
Drawbacks
–Write latency doubles — must wait for both cache and DB
–Cache bloat — data that's written but never read still occupies cache
–Not suitable for high write-frequency workloads (counters, events)
Write-behind (Write-back) — Write to cache only; the DB write is deferred and done asynchronously in the background. The fastest write pattern — caller returns immediately after cache is updated.
Write path
App
▶
1. write cache
Cache (Redis)
returns instantly
◀
returns to caller
App (returns)
▶
async (later)
Database
flushed async
Step by step
1
App writes to cache only — returns instantly
No DB call on the hot path. Response time is sub-millisecond. High throughput.
2
Write is queued (dirty buffer / Redis list)
The cache marks the entry as "dirty" — needs to be flushed to DB. Often implemented as a Redis list or in-memory queue.
3
Background thread flushes to DB in batches
A scheduled job or event loop drains the queue and writes to DB. Can batch 100s of writes into one SQL statement (huge DB load reduction).
Java — write-behind with Redis list + @Scheduled flush
@Service
public class WriteBehindProductService {

  private static final String QUEUE_KEY = "writequeue:products";

  // Write to cache + enqueue — returns instantly, no DB call
  public Product save(Product product) {
    redisTemplate.opsForValue().set(
      "product:" + product.getId(), product, Duration.ofHours(1));

    // Enqueue ID for async DB flush
    redisTemplate.opsForList().rightPush(QUEUE_KEY, product.getId());
    return product;   // returns immediately — DB not touched yet
  }

  // Background flush every 5 seconds — drains queue, batch-writes to DB
  @Scheduled(fixedDelay = 5000)
  public void flushToDB() {
    List<Long> ids = new ArrayList<>();
    Long id;
    while ((id = redisTemplate.opsForList().leftPop(QUEUE_KEY)) != null) {
      ids.add(id);
    }
    if (ids.isEmpty()) return;

    // Bulk fetch from cache, batch insert/update DB
    List<Product> toSave = ids.stream()
      .map(i -> redisTemplate.opsForValue().get("product:" + i))
      .filter(Objects::nonNull)
      .collect(Collectors.toList());
    productRepo.saveAll(toSave);  // one batch SQL call
  }
}
Advantages
+Ultra-low write latency — fastest possible write path
+Batch DB writes reduce DB load dramatically
+Ideal for high-frequency writes: analytics events, view counters, session data
Drawbacks
–Data loss risk — if cache crashes before flush, writes in queue are lost
–Eventual consistency only — DB is always behind cache by flush interval
–Complex failure handling and ordering guarantees
Read-through — The cache layer itself loads data from DB on a miss. The application only ever talks to the cache — it never calls the DB directly. The cache acts as a transparent proxy.
Read path (cache miss)
App
▶
1. read(key)
Cache layer
MISS — fetches itself
▶
2. load from DB
Database
returns row
◀
3. store + return
App
gets data
Step by step
1
App calls cache.get(key) — only ever talks to cache
App has no reference to the DB. All data access goes through the cache abstraction.
M
Cache miss — cache calls its configured loader
Cache provider invokes a CacheLoader / loader function that you registered at startup. The cache handles the DB call internally.
3
Cache stores result, returns to app
From the app's perspective it's a single synchronous call. The miss/load/store is invisible.
H
Next read — cache hit, loader never invoked
Subsequent reads for the same key return instantly. Loader only called again after TTL expiry or explicit eviction.
Java — Caffeine LoadingCache (read-through)
// LoadingCache: on miss, Caffeine calls the loader automatically
// App never calls productRepo directly
@Bean
public LoadingCache<Long, Product> productCache() {
  return Caffeine.newBuilder()
    .expireAfterWrite(10, TimeUnit.MINUTES)
    .maximumSize(10_000)
    .build(id -> productRepo.findById(id)  // ← the loader
        .orElseThrow(NotFoundException::new));
}

// App service — only calls cache, never DB directly
@Service
public class ProductService {
  @Autowired LoadingCache<Long, Product> productCache;

  public Product get(Long id) {
    return productCache.get(id);  // miss? loader fires automatically
  }

  // Async read-through variant (non-blocking)
  public CompletableFuture<Product> getAsync(Long id) {
    AsyncLoadingCache<Long, Product> asyncCache = Caffeine.newBuilder()
      .buildAsync(k -> productRepo.findByIdAsync(k));
    return asyncCache.get(id);
  }
}
Advantages
+App code is clean — no if/else cache check logic anywhere
+Cache manages its own population — no risk of forgetting to populate
+Loader deduplication — concurrent misses trigger one DB call, not N
Drawbacks
–Cache provider must know how to load data (tightly coupled)
–Harder to test — cache and loader are bundled together
–First request still has full miss latency
Spring @Cacheable — A declarative abstraction over cache-aside. Spring AOP intercepts the annotated method, checks the backing store (Redis, Caffeine, etc.) first, and only invokes the actual method on a cache miss. The backing store is swappable via config.
How Spring AOP intercepts the method
Caller
▶
invoke method
Spring AOP Proxy
intercepts call
▶
check store
Cache Store
Redis / Caffeine
▶
miss only
Real method (DB)
invoked on miss
All four annotations explained
R
@Cacheable — read
Cache-aside for reads. Checks cache first; executes method only on miss. The return value is stored in cache keyed by the SpEL key expression.
W
@CachePut — write-through
Always executes the method AND updates the cache. Used on save/update. The return value overwrites whatever was in cache for that key.
E
@CacheEvict — invalidate
Removes a key (or all entries with allEntries=true) after the method runs. Used on delete or batch-update operations.
C
@Caching — combine multiple operations
Groups multiple cache annotations on one method. E.g. evict one cache and put into another in the same call.
Spring Boot — all annotations + Redis TTL config
// ── Enable caching ──────────────────────────────────────────
@SpringBootApplication
@EnableCaching
public class App {}

// ── Configure Redis backing store with per-cache TTL ────────
@Bean
public CacheManager cacheManager(RedisConnectionFactory cf) {
  Map<String, RedisCacheConfiguration> configs = Map.of(
    "products", RedisCacheConfiguration.defaultCacheConfig()
        .entryTtl(Duration.ofMinutes(10)),
    "inventory", RedisCacheConfiguration.defaultCacheConfig()
        .entryTtl(Duration.ofSeconds(30))
  );
  return RedisCacheManager.builder(cf)
      .withInitialCacheConfigurations(configs).build();
}

// ── Service with all four annotations ──────────────────────
@Service
public class ProductService {

  @Cacheable(value="products", key="#id",
             unless="#result == null")
  public Product findById(Long id) {
    return repo.findById(id).orElse(null);
  }

  @CachePut(value="products", key="#result.id")
  public Product save(Product p) { return repo.save(p); }

  @CacheEvict(value="products", key="#id")
  public void delete(Long id)    { repo.deleteById(id); }

  // Evict entire cache (e.g. after bulk import)
  @CacheEvict(value="products", allEntries=true)
  public void importAll(List<Product> list) { repo.saveAll(list); }

  // @Caching: evict two caches at once
  @Caching(evict = {
    @CacheEvict(value="products", key="#p.id"),
    @CacheEvict(value="inventory", key="#p.id")
  })
  public void deleteWithStock(Product p) { ... }
}
Advantages
+Zero boilerplate — three annotations replace 20 lines of if/else
+Backing store is swappable (Redis → Caffeine → EhCache) via config only
+SpEL key expressions allow fine-grained cache key control
Drawbacks
–Only works via Spring proxy — self-calls within same class bypass cache
–No built-in stampede protection — must add manually with locks
–Default key collisions possible if not using SpEL explicitly
Hibernate 2nd-level cache — A cache that lives between the ORM and the DB. It is shared across all Hibernate sessions in the same JVM (or cluster). JPA entities and JPQL query results are cached at the ORM level — no manual Redis calls needed.
Cache levels in Hibernate
Session (L1)
per-request, always on
▶
miss
2nd-Level Cache
EhCache / Hazelcast
▶
miss
Database
source of truth
Step by step
L1
Session (L1) cache — always on, per-request
Every Hibernate Session has its own L1 cache. Entities loaded within the same session/transaction are never re-fetched from DB. Cleared at session close.
L2
2nd-level cache — opt-in, shared across sessions
Annotate an entity with @Cache. Hibernate stores the entity by ID in EhCache/Hazelcast. All sessions share it. Survives request boundaries.
Q
Query cache — caches JPQL/HQL result sets
Cache the list of IDs returned by a query. Subsequent identical queries return the same IDs, then hit L2 for each entity. Requires both @QueryHints and 2LC enabled.
Java — Hibernate 2nd-level cache with EhCache
// application.yml
spring.jpa.properties.hibernate.cache.use_second_level_cache: true
spring.jpa.properties.hibernate.cache.use_query_cache: true
spring.jpa.properties.hibernate.cache.region.factory_class:
  org.hibernate.cache.jcache.JCacheRegionFactory

// pom.xml dependency
<dependency>
  <groupId>org.hibernate.orm</groupId>
  <artifactId>hibernate-jcache</artifactId>
</dependency>
<dependency>
  <groupId>org.ehcache</groupId>
  <artifactId>ehcache</artifactId>
</dependency>

// Entity — opt in with @Cache
@Entity
@Cache(usage = CacheConcurrencyStrategy.READ_WRITE)  // shared cache region
public class Product {
  @Id private Long id;

  // Cache collection associations too
  @OneToMany(mappedBy = "product")
  @Cache(usage = CacheConcurrencyStrategy.READ_WRITE)
  private List<Review> reviews;
}

// Cacheable JPQL query — caches result IDs
@Repository
public interface ProductRepo extends JpaRepository<Product, Long> {

  @QueryHints(@QueryHint(
    name = "org.hibernate.cacheable", value = "true"))
  List<Product> findByCategory(String category);
}

// Concurrency strategies
// READ_ONLY    — immutable data (best performance)
// NONSTRICT_RW — rarely updated, stale reads acceptable
// READ_WRITE   — updated data, uses soft locks (most common)
// TRANSACTIONAL— full transactional cache (JTA required)
Advantages
+Transparent — no explicit cache.get/set anywhere in app code
+Automatic invalidation on entity updates within the same app
+Works at the ORM level — caches entity graphs and associations
Drawbacks
–Clustered cache required in multi-node setups (Hazelcast/Infinispan)
–Stale if DB is modified by non-Hibernate code (native SQL, other services)
–Query cache + L2 interaction is subtle — easy to misconfigure
Side-by-side comparison
PatternWho manages cache?Read latencyWrite latencyConsistencyData loss riskBest use case
Cache-aside Application code Fast on hit Fast (DB only) Eventual (TTL) None Read-heavy; mixed workloads; default choice
Write-through Application code Always fast Slower (2 writes) Strong None Read-heavy data written infrequently
Write-behind Application code Always fast Fastest Eventual only Yes (pre-flush) Counters, events, session writes, analytics
Read-through Cache provider Fast on hit Fast (DB only) Eventual (TTL) None Clean app code; Caffeine LoadingCache
Spring @Cacheable Spring AOP Fast on hit Normal Eventual (TTL) None Any Spring service; zero boilerplate
Hibernate L2 ORM / Hibernate Fast on hit Normal ORM-managed None Entity/association caching; JPA-heavy apps
When to use which
1
Default choice → Spring @Cacheable (cache-aside under the hood)
For 90% of Spring Boot use cases. Annotate service methods, plug in Redis or Caffeine. No boilerplate, swappable backing store.
2
Write-heavy, strong consistency needed → Write-through (@CachePut)
Use alongside @Cacheable for the read path. @CachePut ensures every save also updates the cache immediately.
3
Ultra-high write throughput, eventual consistency OK → Write-behind
View counters, like counts, event ingestion, real-time metrics. Accept data loss risk on crash; ensure Redis persistence (AOF) is on.
4
Entity + association caching in JPA apps → Hibernate L2
When you want ORM-level caching without writing Redis calls. Works best for reference data (categories, config, lookup tables) that rarely changes.
5
Multi-pattern: combine them
Typical production stack: Caffeine L1 (in-process, ~100ns) + Redis L2 (distributed, ~1ms) + Hibernate L2 (entity graphs) + @Cacheable as the glue. Each layer catches a different type of miss.
